transcript:
Jacob Creech: All right, so we are here to talk about Anchor, where Anchor is today and where we kind of see Anchor going in the future. Uh, so for those do not know me, my name is Jacob Creech. I do Developer Relations here at Solana Foundation.

Robert Chen: Uh, my name is Robert, and, um, I'm the CEO and auditor at OtterSec.

Jacob Creech: Yes, so as Developer Relations, one of the things that I really care about with Anchor and with Solana in general is I want to make sure that we have the absolute best developer experience, tooling, and also the the most optimi- optimal way of building Solana smart contracts or programs, uh, today. And so that's a lot of what I want to see on Anchor, and we have a lot of work to do, but we'll talk about that a little bit today.

Robert Chen: Yeah. So, I said this at MEV Camp actually, um, but one thing that we really hate as auditors is framework proliferation. Um, and in the past one or two years, one thing that we saw was every protocol, or not every protocol, but a lot of protocols designing their own frameworks. Um, you know, I think we saw two or three custom frameworks launch, uh, you know, in the past two years. Uh, one thing that we're really excited to do when working, uh, on Anchor is to take all of those learnings from these separate frameworks and bring them into Anchor. Um, you know, having different frameworks increases the complexity, not just for developers, but also for auditors. Um, it takes a lot of time to learn the nuances of each different framework and also to understand where it can go wrong. Um, you know, if you have two and three different two or three different frameworks, you need to learn each one individually. Um, and one reason we're so excited to work with Jacob here from Foundation on Anchor is, uh, because we can consolidate everything and make Anchor, you know, the the one framework to to rule them all.

Jacob Creech: Yes. And with that, we are excited to announce that if you you can go out and try, uh, an Anchor 1.0 release candidate right now. Uh, it is actually released. Uh, don't check the release time. It'll be very fun.

Robert Chen: It was like 20 minutes ago.

Jacob Creech: Yes. But we're going to talk a little bit about what goes into the 1.0 release. Uh, please try it out, give us some feedback when it uh before we can actually make it a full 1.0 release. It's part of a release candidate, right? Uh, but the idea for Anchor 1.0 is it is a step in the direction of what we just said, right? It it is a stable version, uh, I know a lot of people have had issues with Anchor in the past, it is a stable version that you can rely on to build Solana programs for now and for the foreseeable future. So let's go into some of the changes that we can see on it. Uh, go for it.

Robert Chen: Okay. So if you're a developer, maybe you don't care that much about this, but if you're an auditor, hopefully you're super excited. One of the things that has been super annoying in Solana development in general, but also in Anchor, is duplicate accounts, and specifically duplicate mutable accounts. Uh, this is a super common footgun, um, in in Solana where you can pass the same account multiple times. And in Anchor what this does is only the last account persists. Um, and and the reason for this is a little technical, but essentially the Anchor exit handler will serialize each account in order, um, but because it's doing these sequential serializations, only the last, uh, only the last version of that duplicate account gets serialized back. Which means, for example, if you have like a token account, um, and you're transferring tokens, uh, only the the the second version of that token account gets persisted back into, uh, into memory. Um, and this is a huge problem because a lot of Anchor developers don't know about this, um, and it's a very common footgun that we see as auditors that we have to manually point out, um, and and remind developers. And it's it's been the cause of quite a few critical issues that we've seen. Um, so in Anchor 1.0, this will no longer be a thing. Um, you know, you can always opt in to duplicate mutable accounts, but by default, um, you will or the the framework will handle this edge case for you. It will prevent you from passing in two accounts, um, of the same pubkey into two separate, um, into two separate Anchor accounts.

Jacob Creech: Yes. And as part of making Anchor stable, we're also going to be upgrading Borsh. Uh, so Borsh is on currently in the current version of Anchor of 0.32.1, it's kind of like on a three-year-old version of Anchor, uh, at this point. And it actually can't work today with Solana SDK 2.0 or higher. Uh, so we actually had to had to upgrade it to a v1. Uh, so this will not only allow us to make sure that we are stable from now into the future, but it will also make sure that, uh, we don't have all these weird dependency conflicts just suddenly in a 1.0 because no one wants that kind of issue. And on dependency conflicts, one of the things that we wanted to also talk about with Anchor v1 is that Anchor, part of v1 is it's not just a framework anymore, but it's also a toolchain in itself. We have a bunch of different tools within the ecosystem that people use today. We have Surfpool, right? A lot of people, if you're not using Surfpool, go use it, it's awesome. Uh, but Surfpool is an awesome little, uh, Solana test validator which is a direct upgrade from the current Solana test validator. We have a lot of people using LiteSVM or Mollusk or a bunch of other tools in the ecosystem. A lot of people use Verifiable Builds. Uh, what we did to this time around is we packaged all those tools all under Anchor as the toolchain. Uh, and not only can you use Anchor to build Anchor framework programs, but you can also use Anchor with all these tools with, uh, your native programs, your Pinocchio, whatever. And it it should build and work just fine. Uh, what's also great about this is in Anchor 1.0, everybody kind of talks about like, oh, I ran into a dependency issue with this library or dependency issue with that library. Uh, what we kind of did with, uh, this is instead of having this mix and matching of a Solana CLI version, a platform tools version, and Anchor version, you would just have to know the Anchor version. Because in, uh, An- Anchor 1.0, we removed the requirement of the Solana CLI, and we also have the platform tools ti- version tied to the Anchor version as well. So you don't have to worry about that. It will just work whenever you build. Uh, that's part of being stable in Anchor today.

Robert Chen: Yeah, I mean, this that was super cool. I mean, I feel like we can't overstate how amazing this is. I mean, there's been countless times where we're trying to audit a program and I'm like, how do I compile this thing? Um, because of all the different, uh, versions of tools that you need. And, um, yeah, we're going to talk a bit more about the toolchain later, but this is I think a super great addition to to Anchor.

Jacob Creech: So as part of getting to an Anchor 1.0, we did a lot of work, uh, since we took it over this year, to actually get it into a maintained state. Uh, so we did, as you can see from this graph, you can basically see when both Robert and I started working on it, uh, especially on the issues closed part. Uh, thank thank you also Henry, uh, if he's in the crowd here, uh, for helping us out. He helped us a ton as well. Uh, but we wanted to make it into a maintained state so that if people are making new contributions or they want to make an issue or they want to contribute back, they can. It's a lot easier. So we have under a hundred under what like a under a hundred PRs open now.

Robert Chen: Yeah, we we got under a hundred PRs finally. Um, it was like 300 for the longest time.

Jacob Creech: Yeah, it was it was a big cleanup. We moved a bunch of things in that would have been sitting forever. Uh, but we really, if you are in the crowd and you had problems contributing back to Anchor, please open an issue, we can talk it out. Uh, we are here to maintain it and make it better.

Robert Chen: Another thing that we've been doing is trying to involve the community more. Um, I think a big part of wha- what makes Anchor successful is the fact that everyone uses it, and we want to be mindful, um, of of the impact it has on everyone's development cycle. Um, if you have thoughts or feedback or ideas for where you want Anchor 1.0 or 2.0 to be, uh, you should join our community calls. We host them once once a month. Uh, we skipped it for this month because we're at Breakpoint, but next month will be on on January 14th, and we we host it, um, around that time every month. Um, and both Jacob and I and and other people from the team are there, and and we want again to to hear your feedback, to to hear what you have to say, to to understand your pain points so we know, uh, what we should be doing to make it better. Um, and you know, I th- I think as as Jacob said, uh, you know, we are really putting in a lot more effort to maintain this thing, and we want to engage the community and understand how we can make this the best version of the framework it can be. Sort of on that note, um, a lot of people don't know this, but Anchor actually has been audited. Um, we we audited, um, some of the the previous versions, and we're auditing the 1.0 release. Um, if you want, you can go to, uh, the URL on screen to to see the audits. And we're also working on the security lifecycle for for Anchor as a whole. So we launched a bug bounty program, um, where you can email your issues or ideas. Uh, actually they go directly to Jacob and I, so we will see the issues if you have any, and we'll I mean, I actually like check my email and respond to them, uh, relatively quickly. Um, so I think the point here is that, you know, if you have concerns or ideas or or anything that you see on Anchor, you know, we're we're here to look at them and and here to help. Um, and we really want to to make Anchor the best it can be.

Jacob Creech: On that note, we wanted to also talk about, so we talked about Anchor of where it is today, right? We wanted to talk about where we want Anchor to go in the future. So there's a whole bunch of things that we talked about at the beginning of like, hey, we want to make sure it's the mo- best developer experience. It is able to do all the things that these different frameworks are doing or that are being built today are doing already. Uh, it can have good defaults. It can have, uh, it's optimized but easy to move to something low-level like Pinocchio rather than there were some times or some teams in the ecosystem that spent eight plus months moving their programs from An- Anchor or native to Pinocchio to get to get the extra juice out uh of their program on-chain. And so we want to make that easy. So as part of that, the first thing that we want to do is we want to switch out, uh, the internals of Solana program with Pinocchio. You may have seen me talk about this ev- every once in a while on socials, uh, but that is like really important to us because like, one, it gets us almost no dependencies, but two, it also makes it very easy to optimize. And then we're going to also make instead of Borsh and a bunch of the bunch of the other work that goes in today, uh, we're going to make zero copy a first-class citizen within Anchor v2 so that you don't have to worry so much uh whenever you're doing the loading of accounts or the the writing of accounts, that sort of thing. Uh, so that is a big effort that's going on right now, uh, so that we can get this v2 out the door and make sure that, hey, if you want to use Pinocchio, you have bo- you have both the optimization that Pinocchio provides but also the best developer experience that you know and love from Anchor.

Robert Chen: One important theme for us around Anchor v2 is also making it extensible. Um, you know, we want this to be the framework that all developers can use regardless of your skill level. And, you know, th- we have worked with developers who have built their initial programs on Anchor, they realized they wanted to get more control over the the framework, and then they couldn't get that with Anchor. Um, so one of the things that we're adding, um, along along this theme is, uh, a better trait-based a better trait-based system where if you're a developer, you can override parts of Anchor, um, to get exactly the functionality you want. So for example, if you don't want to use the default entry point and you don't want to use the default account parsing, you can now override that in Anchor v2. You know, if if you really want to optimize, for example, if you're a prop AMM and you really want to optimize your oracle updates, you should be able to do that in in Anchor v2 where you only have to opt into the parts that you actually want to. Um, and the goal here is and the hope here is that regardl- regardless of your level as a as a developer, you can use Anchor, um, and it can be the framework that you stick with. Another thing that we're really excited to announce is Anchor Fuzz. Um, so we want to bring native fuzz testing to the Anchor framework. Um, fuzzing is an immensely useful tool, um, and we think every developer should be able to to access it and write it similar to how you might write a unit test. Um, so as part of this, we're adding, um, this is a this is a screenshot from the the test instance we have, but we're adding a suite of CLI tools where you can create a harness, you can run the harness, you can check the coverage, you know, you can check your crashes, um, and you can see, you know, sort of get all the all the features you expect of a normal fuzzing tool. Um, we don't have that much time today here, but if you're interested in learning more, we're actually going to or someone from the team is going to be giving a talk at the Dev Cave, uh, tomorrow. Um, I don't know exactly what time, but he will be, uh, he'll be there. Uh, also the Dev Cave is a little hard to find. So if you don't know where that is, uh, it's like you go into Breakpoint and then you take a hard left and it's on floor 1.5. So not floor 2, it's like 1.5 and it's like kind of stuck in the corner somewhere. Um, we're also hosting a CTF there. So if you want to compete, uh, and you want to win some prizes, I think the the top prize is 30 SOL, which is like, uh, $4,000? Um, uh, but yeah, if you want to if you want to compete and win some prizes, definitely encourage you to go there and and and check it out. Um.

Jacob Creech: So on also on Anchor Fuzz, it's part of, if you remember I'm talking about making Anchor also a toolchain that you can use to build programs, this is just going to be put in, it so this is not specific to 2.0. We'll also add it to a 1.0 eventually when it gets built and ful- fully released. So you'll have like a 1.1 or 1.2 or something at that point. It's just building the best toolset for you as a developer to build your programs.

Robert Chen: Yeah, one thing we need to figure out is the the disam- disambiguation of the naming, right? Because there's Anchor the framework and then we al- now we have Anchor the toolchain. Um, but that's a tomorrow problem. Uh, another thing that we're excited to talk about is linting. Um, so this is similar to to the fuzzing where we want to bring more uh we want to increase the water level of security uh in the Solana ecosystem. And adding more features to the to the Anchor toolchain is is one of the best ways to do that. Um, so we're also excited to announce Anchor Linting, which is a Dylint-based linting framework. Um, essentially it's static analysis. So we have hardcoded some rules, um, for for Anchor programs. Um, you know, there's for example, maybe you want to check for reloading an account af- after a CPI, which is a pretty common footgun that a lot of new developers miss. Um, we want to write these rules and then enable everyone to run them as part of their product lifecycle. So before you submit your code to an audit, you should be able to run Anchor Lint, you know, pull down the latest rules, see how it looks, and and fix them before, you know, you even submit it to an audit. Um, another thing that we want to do with this is to make it open to everyone. Um, so you don't have to use our rules. Anyone in the community can contribute rules and and add rules, you know, either to our repository, uh, or even self-hosted. And Anchor Lint will give you ways to pull down these community rules, um, and run them on your project. So if you have ideas for interesting bugs that you think we should be looking for, you know, you can post your own rules and and everyone in the community can use them.

Jacob Creech: Cool. So thank you for coming to learn about Anchor. If you have any questions, feel free to message us on Discord. You can see our two, uh, usernames right there. Uh, or join the Discord for Anchor, join the Anchor Contributors channel and start messaging us. We're happy to have a chat on any new features or any problems that you might have building with Anchor, especially as we build towards v2. But thank you everyone.

enrichment.generated_query: Anchor 1.0 Official Announcement Blog OR Technical Docs Solana Foundation Solana Breakpoint 2025

tags.impact_reason: The announcement of the Anchor 1.0 release candidate marks a critical milestone for Solana's development infrastructure, delivering long-awaited stability and standardizing the framework used by the vast majority of ecosystem protocols.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a futuristic, high-tech anchor as the semantic anchor because the product is named 'Anchor' and the story is about providing a stable, secure foundation (v1.0) for Solana developers. The metallic, 'bare-metal' aesthetic reflects the v2 performance goals. I integrated the text '1.0' diegetically, etched onto the anchor like a version number on hardware, to highlight the major stable release mentioned in the headline.
visual_idea.description: A massive, sleek, futuristic anchor made of brushed titanium dominates the frame, its form suggesting immense weight and stability. It rests securely on a dark, reflective digital plane. Intricate cyan and magenta circuitry patterns glow and pulse along its hard edges. Laser-etched onto the main shank of the anchor, the version number '1.0' emits a soft, internal white light. In the deep background, a vast digital void is pierced by volumetric light shafts, revealing faint, out-of-focus data streams drifting like cosmic dust.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A massive, sleek, futuristic anchor, meticulously crafted from brushed titanium, dominates the frame, its colossal form conveying immense weight and unwavering stability. It rests securely upon a dark, highly reflective digital plane, mirroring its grandeur. Along its hard edges, intricate cyan and magenta circuitry patterns glow and pulse with a vibrant, high-contrast luminescence. Laser-etched deep into the main shank of the anchor, the version number '1.0' emits a soft, internal white light, appearing as an integral part of its structure. In the deep background, a vast digital void is dramatically pierced by powerful, cinematic volumetric light shafts, subtly revealing faint, out-of-focus data streams drifting like cosmic dust. Composed for a vertical 3:4 aspect ratio, emphasizing monumental height, epic scale, and expansive negative space at the top to convey immense scale and gravitas, with a strong emphasis on dramatic lighting and deep shadows. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, ultra-photorealistic, high dynamic range, cinematic lighting.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a futuristic, high-tech anchor as the semantic anchor because the product is named 'Anchor' and the story is about providing a stable, secure foundation (v1.0) for Solana developers. The metallic, 'bare-metal' aesthetic reflects the v2 performance goals. I integrated the text '1.0' diegetically, etched onto the anchor like a version number on hardware, to highlight the major stable release mentioned in the headline.
visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on the joint where the anchor's fluke meets the shank. The texture of the brushed titanium is highly detailed, with sharp, precision-engineered edges. A network of glowing cyan circuits is embedded in the metal, pulsing with light, suggesting active data flow. The lighting is dramatic, catching the metallic highlights and casting deep, contrasting shadows, emphasizing the anchor's robust and high-tech construction.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro shot focusing on the joint where the anchor's fluke meets the shank. The brushed titanium texture is highly detailed, featuring a realistic metallic sheen, sharp, precision-engineered edges, and microscopic surface imperfections that catch the light. Embedded in the metal is a network of glowing cyan circuits, pulsing with an intense, active data flow. The scene is illuminated by dramatic, high-contrast cinematic volumetric lighting, with a single, focused light source creating sharp metallic highlights and casting deep, well-defined shadows that emphasize the anchor's robust and high-tech construction. The composition is square (1:1), with a symmetrical, centrally focused, tight macro frame, filling the entire canvas with intricate texture and detail. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, ultra-photorealistic, cinematic contrast, realistic materials, microscopic details.

editorial.headline: Anchor 1.0 Hardens Solana Security; v2 Targets Bare-Metal Performance
editorial.standfirst: Solana's premier framework releases a stable 1.0 version to eliminate critical serialization vulnerabilities, while the v2 roadmap unveils a strategic shift to the Pinocchio library for high-frequency optimization.
key_takeaways: **Anchor 1.0 Release:** Patches the critical "duplicate mutable accounts" exploit and stabilizes the toolchain by decoupling it from the Solana CLI versioning.
**Pinocchio Integration:** Anchor v2 will replace `solana-program` with **Pinocchio**, enabling zero-copy deserialization and significantly lower compute unit (CU) usage.
**Native Security Tools:** The launch of **Anchor Fuzz** and **Anchor Lint** integrates audit-standard static analysis and fuzzing directly into the developer workflow.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
For years, the Solana ecosystem has faced a quiet fragmentation: while **Anchor** served as the onboarding standard, high-performance protocols—specifically order book exchanges and market makers—often abandoned it for custom, "bare metal" Rust implementations to shave off compute units (CUs). The announcement of **Anchor 1.0** and the roadmap for **v2** at Breakpoint 2025 signals a strategic pivot to recapture this institutional volume. By stabilizing the core framework and integrating the ultra-lightweight **Pinocchio** library, the Solana Foundation and **OtterSec** are effectively merging developer convenience with high-frequency performance.

The immediate alpha lies in the security hardening of Anchor 1.0. The release finally patches the "duplicate mutable accounts" vulnerability—a notorious serialization flaw where only the last instance of a duplicated account was persisted, leading to potential state corruption in complex DeFi transactions. Coupled with the launch of **Anchor Lint** (a Dylint-based static analyzer) and **Anchor Fuzz**, the framework is institutionalizing audit-grade security directly into the CI/CD pipeline. For VCs and allocators, this reduces the technical risk profile of early-stage Solana projects, as common "footguns" are now caught by the compiler rather than a post-mortem.

Looking ahead, the integration of **Pinocchio** in Anchor v2 is a game-changer for protocol architects. Currently, Anchor’s reliance on the standard `solana-program` crate imposes a binary size and CU overhead that is unacceptable for optimized MEV strategies. By replacing this with Pinocchio’s zero-dependency, zero-copy architecture, Anchor v2 will allow developers to override default entry points and account parsing logic. This "trait-based" extensibility means proprietary trading firms and complex AMMs can optimize critical paths without forking the entire framework, unifying the ecosystem under a single, performant standard.

Finally, Anchor has evolved from a mere library into a comprehensive **toolchain**. The inclusion of **Surfpool**—a next-generation test validator capable of forking mainnet state instantly—alongside verified builds and the removal of strict Solana CLI dependencies, creates a deterministic build environment. This standardization is crucial for institutional adoption, as it ensures that the code audited by firms like **OtterSec** is bit-for-bit identical to what is deployed on-chain, eliminating the "dependency hell" that has previously plagued Solana development.
